title: CloudFront Cache Keys, Origin Requests, TTLs, and Invalidation · Language Lens: Cache Key Identity
summary: A cache key includes every viewer header, authorization is omitted, the origin receives different data than the cache varies on, and releases rely on global invalidation as routine deployment. Predict the representation, storage, trust, cost, and recovery evidence before inspecting a model. Planned artifact: Add and verify the cache key identity increment in cache-key, forwarding, and freshness contract. Learners progress through prediction, explicit theory, changed-case practice, causal debugg…
objectives: Include only representation-changing path, query, header, cookie, encoding, and authorization dimensions in the cache key.
Migrate OAI to OAC with temporary dual authorization, deployed distribution evidence, negative tests, monitoring, and policy rollback.
Preserve previously learned constraints and attach observable changed-case evidence.

steps.title: S3, CloudFront, and Go Predict Cache Key Identity · Theory Cache Key Identity
steps.instruction:
theory case — A theory Go file-delivery team handles case 4848 during a regional dependency incident. Build deterministic Go evidence to align cache identity, origin forwarding, TTL, versioned names, and urgent invalidation; keep browser execution deterministic and move AWS claims to an authorized transfer gate; then cancel halfway through and prove every body, part, and goroutine reaches a terminal state. Browser code uses pure values and state transitions only. AWS SDK packages, networks, S3, CloudFront, KMS, IAM, WAF, databases, credentials, host state, race and leak checks, load, faults, restore, billing, and production behavior require explicit authorized transfer gates.

Before reading, predict which action preserves the Cache Key Identity contract for the supplied data.
